elements.sent-2411-113: For nonnegative compactly supported smooth functions $f_i:\mathbb R^d\rightarrow \mathbb R$, $i\in \{1,2,\dots,n\}$, we have: \begin{equation}\|\mathcal A^n(f_1,f_2,\dots ,f_n)(x)\|_{L^1(\mathbb R^d)} = \int_{\mathbb R^d}\int_{\mathbb S^{nd-1}}f_1(x-y_1)f_2(x-y_2)\cdots f_n(x-y_n)d\sigma_{nd-1} (y_1,y_2,\dots ,y_n)dx = \int_{\mathbb R^d}f_n(x)\int_{\mathbb S^{nd-1}} \prod_{l=1}^{n-1} f_l(x-(y_l-y_n)) d\sigma_{nd-1} (y_1,y_2,\dots ,y_n)dx = \int_{\mathbb R^d}f_n(x)\int_{\mathbb R^{(n-1)d}} \prod_{l=1}^{n-1} f_l(x-z_l) d\sigma_{nd-1} (-)(z_1,z_2,\dots ,z_{n-1})dx = \int_{\mathbb R^d}f_n(x)\int_{\mathbb R^{(n-1)d}}\prod_{l=1}^{n-1} f_l(x-z_l) \frac{d\sigma_{nd-1} (-)}{dz_1dz_2\cdots dz_{n-1}} dz_1dz_2\cdots dz_{n-1}dx \lesssim \int_{\mathbb R^d}f_n(x)\int_{\mathbb R^{(n-1)d}}f_1(x-z_1)f_2(x-z_2)\cdots f_{n-1}(x-z_{n-1})dz_1dz_2\cdots dz_{n-1}dx = \|f_1\|_{L^1(\mathbb R^d)}\|f_1\|_{L^1(\mathbb R^d)}\cdots \| f_1\|_{L^1(\mathbb R^d)}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-115: One can see that when $n\geq 2$ the bounds obtained are nontrivial when $r<1$; however, the bounds for $r\ge 1$ has can be deduced by Minkowski's integral inequality via a straightforward way: \begin{equation}\lVert\mathcal A^n(f) \rVert_{L^r} = \bigg(\int_{\mathbb R^d}\Big\lvert\int_{\mathbb S^{nd-1}}f_1(x-y_1)\cdots f_n(x-y_n)d\sigma_{nd-1} (y_1,y_2,\dots ,y_n)\Big\rvert ^rdx\bigg)^{\frac{1}{r}} \leq \int_{\mathbb S^{nd-1}}\bigg(\int_{\mathbb R^d} \lvert f_1(x-y_1)\cdots f_n(x-y_n)\rvert ^rdx\bigg)^{\frac{1}{r}}d\sigma_{nd-1} (y_1,y_2,\dots ,y_n) = \int_{\mathbb S^{d-1}}\bigg(\int_{\mathbb R^d} \lvert f_1(x-y_1)\rvert^{p_1} dx\bigg)^{\frac{1}{p_1}}\cdots\bigg(\int_{\mathbb R^d}\lvert f_n(x-y_n)\rvert^{p_n} dx\bigg)^{\frac{1}{p_n}}d\sigma_{d-1}(y) = \lVert f_1\rVert_{L^{p_1}}\cdots\lVert f_n\rVert_{L^{p_n}}.\end{equation}.

elements.formula-2411-072: \begin{equation}\label{bound1} \Big\|M_{N}^{i_1,\dots,i_n}\Big(\{f_{1,l}\}_{|l|\leq N}, \dots,\{f_{n,l}\}_{|l|\leq N}\Big)\Big\|_{L^r(l^\infty)} = \Big\|\sup_{|l|\leq N}A_{N}^{i_1,\dots,i_n}\big(f_{1,l}*\psi_{i_1+l},\dots,f_{n,l}*\psi_{i_n+l}\big)\Big\|_{L^r} \lesssim \Big\|\sup_{|l|\leq N}A_{N}^{i_1,\dots,i_n}(M(f_{1,l}),\dots, M(f_{n,l}))\Big\|_{L^r} \lesssim \Big\|A_{N}^{i_1,\dots,i_n}(M(\sup_{|l|\leq N}f_{1,l}),\dots, M(\sup_{|l|\leq N}f_{n,l})\Big\|_{L^r} \leq A_{N}(p_1,\dots,p_n,r)\|\sup_{|l|\leq N}f_{1,l}\|_{L^{p_1}},\dots, \|\sup_{|l|\leq N}f_{n,l}\|_{L^{p_n}} = A_{N}(p_1,\dots,p_n,r)\|\{f_{1,l}\}_{|l|\leq N}\|_{L^{p_1}(l^\infty)},\dots, \|\{f_{n,l}\}_{|l|\leq N}\|_{L^{p_n}(l^\infty)}. \end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-076: \begin{equation}A_{N}(p_1,\dots,p_n,r)^{\min(1,r)} = \Big\|\sup_{|l|\leq N}|A_{2^{-l}}^{i_1,\dots,i_n}|\Big\|^{\min(1,r)}_{L^{p_1}\times\cdots\times L^{p_n}\rightarrow L^r} \leq \Big\| \sum_{m\in I}\mathscr M^B_{lac} \Big\|_{L^{p_1}\times\cdots\times L^{p_n}\rightarrow L^r}^{\min(1,r)}+\Big\|\sum_{i_1,\dots,i_n\geq 1} \sup_{|l|\leq N}|A^{i_1,\dots,i_n}_{2^{-l}}|\Big\|_{L^{p_1}\times \cdots\times L^{p_n}\rightarrow L^r}^{\min(1,r)} \lesssim 1+\sum_{i_1,\dots,i_n\geq 1} \Big\|\sup_{|l|\leq N}|A^{i_1,\dots,i_n}_{2^{-l}}|\Big\|_{L^{p_1}\times \cdots\times L^{p_n}\rightarrow L^r}^{\min(1,r)} \lesssim 1+A_{N}(p_1,\dots,p_n,r)^{\min(\frac{1}{2},\frac{r}{2})}\sum_{i,j\geq 1}C(i_1,\dots,i_n,p_1,\dots,p_n)^{\min(\frac{1}{2},\frac{r}{2})}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-171: Hence, we deduce \begin{equation}A_{\mathcal{N}}(p_1,\dots,p_n,r)\lesssim 1 +\left\{\sum_{i_1.\dots,i_n\geq 1} C(i_1,\dots,i_n,p_1,\dots,p_n)^{r/2}\right\}^{2/r},\quad r<1,\end{equation} and \begin{equation}A_{\mathcal{N}}(p_1,\dots,p_n)\lesssim 1 +\left\{\sum_{i_1.\dots,i_n\geq 1} C(i_1,\dots,i_n,p_1,\dots,p_n)^{1/2}\right\}^2,\quad r\geq 1.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-179: Let $X_1$,$X_2$ be open subsets of $\mathbb R^{n_1}$ and $\mathbb R^{n_2}$ respectively, where $n_1\geq n_2$.
elements.sent-2411-180: Then there exists a unique continuous linear map $\Phi^*:\mathcal D'(X_2)\rightarrow \mathcal D'(X_1)$ such that $\Phi^*u= u\circ \Phi$ for every $u\in \mathcal D'(X_2)$.
elements.sent-2411-181: By the inverse function theorem there is an open neighborhood $Y_1 \subset X_1$ of $x_0$ such that the restriction of $\Phi\oplus \Psi$ to $Y_1$ is a diffeomorphism on an open neighborhood $Y_2$ of $(\Phi(x), \Psi(x))$.
elements.sent-2411-183: If $u\in \mathcal C^0(X_2)$ and $\phi \in \mathcal C_0^\infty (Y_1)$ then a change of variables gives \begin{equation}\int(\Phi^*u)\phi dx =\int u(\Phi(x))\phi(x)dx = \int u(y') \phi(\Upsilon(y))\lvert \det J \Upsilon(y)\rvert dy,\end{equation} where $ detJ \Upsilon(y)$ denotes the determinant of $J \Upsilon(y)$ and we have written $y=(y', y'')\in \mathbb R^{n_2}\oplus\mathbb R^{n_1-n_2}$..
